nativeRange.habitat: Lagoon and seaward reefs, generally near drop-offs and in close association with corals and sponges; reported at depths of roughly 5–40 m, most commonly 20–40 m. Tends to be solitary or, less often, paired.

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding on zooplankton, small crustaceans, and worms in the wild. In aquaria it tends to accept most meaty preparations — frozen mysis, brine shrimp, finely chopped seafood, and quality marine pellets or flakes. Once-daily feeding is generally advised in reef tanks; appetite is typically strong.

avoidWith: small ornamental shrimp (e.g. sexy shrimp, peppermint shrimp)
fireshrimp and other small reef crustaceans
fish of similar shape or color

summary: A vividly colored Indo-Pacific dottyback (Pseudochromidae) generally considered hardy and beginner-friendly. Reef-tolerant but territorial; has been known to harass smaller tankmates and pick at ornamental shrimp.
careNotes:
The Splendid Dottyback is generally considered one of the more straightforward saltwater fish to keep, taking food readily and adapting quickly to captivity. It tends to occupy mid-water near rockwork, retreating into caves and crevices when threatened and patrolling a defended territory once established.

Temperament varies between individuals but is consistently described as semi-aggressive to territorial. Specimens have been known to confront fish three to four times their size, and a single Splendid Dottyback should generally be the only dottyback in the system. Tankmates with similar shape or color are best avoided; otherwise it can coexist with most medium-to-large reef-suitable species.

Though generally regarded as reef-safe with corals and sessile invertebrates, it has been reported to pick at and consume small ornamental shrimp (sexy shrimp, peppermint shrimp, juvenile cleaner shrimp). 'Reef-safe with caution' is the typical hobbyist verdict.

A tightly fitted lid is generally advised — dottybacks are known jumpers and LiveAquaria explicitly recommends a well-sealed top. Provide ample rockwork with caves and overhangs; the species particularly favors drop-offs and crevices that mimic its natural sponge-and-coral habitat.

Cross-source notes: minimum tank size showed wide spread (LiveAquaria 30 US gal / 114 L; Reeflings 55–75 L; Practical Fishkeeping ~180 L; biomesinabox 250 L). Adopted 150 L as a cautious round value reflecting the species' territoriality, ~13 cm adult length, and active swimming style. Adult size: LiveAquaria reports 5.25 in (~13.3 cm), FishBase 13.0 cm TL; Reeflings cites 8 cm, which appears low and was outweighed. Temperature: LiveAquaria/Biomes 72–78°F vs Reeflings 75–81°F; averaged ~23–26°C.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has been reported by some commercial breeders (ORA and similar suppliers occasionally offer captive-bred specimens), but is generally considered difficult in the home aquarium because of strong intraspecific aggression. Like most pseudochromids, the species is believed to be a protogynous hermaphrodite (female-first); pairs are typically formed by introducing a smaller individual to an established larger one. Males have been reported to guard demersal egg balls in crevices.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Used for care level, diet, temperament, reef compatibility, water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), and 30 US gal minimum tank baseline.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Used for accepted scientific name (Manonichthys splendens, Fowler 1931), family (Pseudochromidae), order, max length 13.0 cm TL, depth range 5–40 m, native distribution (Philippines, Indonesia, Australia), and IUCN Least Concern status.

additional.name: Reeflings
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (24–27°C), pH (8.1–8.4), SG (1.020–1.025), reef-safe status, native range (Western Pacific, Indonesia/Philippines), and confirmed protogynous hermaphroditism. Reported size of 8 cm was below the LiveAquaria/FishBase consensus and was outweighed.

additional.name: biomesinabox
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), 30 gal minimum, semi-aggressive temperament (will defend against fish 3–4× larger), reef-safe-with-caution (will consume ornamental shrimp), and explicit recommendation for a well-fitted lid (jumping/escape risk).

additional.name: Whitecorals
additional.notes: European source providing the upper-end tank-size figure (250 L / 65 US gal), 24–26°C temperature, and reef habitat description. Used to bias minVolumeLiters upward from the 114 L hobby-retail baseline.

fish.breedingNotes: Has been bred commercially (e.g. ORA captive-bred specimens exist) but is generally considered difficult in home aquariums due to strong intraspecific aggression. Pairs are usually formed by introducing a smaller individual to a larger established one; males have been reported to guard demersal egg masses laid in crevices.
